steps.text: Engage stakeholders, including laboratory and facility staff and subject matter experts, in committee reviews of work and discussions of past studies of Laboratory-associated infections (LAIs) and other published research.

preconditions.text: When conducting a biological risk assessment

constraints.text: Risk assessment requires careful judgment and is an important responsibility for directors, principal investigators (PI), and institutional leadership/oversight resources.

steps.text: Remove broken glassware using a brush and dustpan, tongs, or forceps.
Decontaminate work surfaces after completion of work and after any spill or splash of potentially infectious material with appropriate disinfectant.
Decontaminate all potentially infectious materials before transport or disposal using an effective method.
Develop and post a spill procedure within the animal facility.
Decontaminate all cultures, stocks, and other potentially infectious materials before disposal using an effective method.
Place materials to be decontaminated outside of the immediate animal room in a durable, leak-proof container and secure for transport.
Disinfect the outer surface of the container prior to moving materials.
Pack materials to be removed from the facility for decontamination in accordance with applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
Implement an effective integrated pest management program.
Provide animal care staff with information on signs and symptoms of disease, occupational medical services, and immunizations.

preconditions.text: If materials are to be decontaminated outside of the immediate animal room
If materials are to be removed from the facility for decontamination

constraints.text: Broken glassware is not handled directly.
All procedures are carefully performed to minimize the creation of aerosols or splatters of infectious materials and waste.
Spills involving infectious materials are contained, decontaminated, and cleaned up by staff who are properly trained and equipped.
The transport container must have a universal biohazard label.
Animals and plants not associated with the work being performed are not permitted in the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.

steps.text: Consider blinding the testing facility to the times the samples were obtained.
Submit samples from uninfected source(s) as negative controls and positive control samples, whenever possible.
Blind the testing facility to sources and timing of sample collection.
Caution the exposed staff member that the clinical utility of non-commercial assays is not the same as licensed tests and must be interpreted with extreme caution.

Work with the principal investigator, veterinarian, or clinician responsible for the source material to determine if testing appropriate samples could help establish if a specific infectious agent was present.

preconditions.text: If a staff member is to be screened with a non-commercial assay based on expert consensus

constraints.text: Negative results may not indicate the absence of a specific infectious agent and should be interpreted with caution.

steps.text: Conduct manipulations of suspect cultures, animal necropsies, and experimental work.

constraints.text: Direct contact of skin or mucous membranes with infectious materials, accidental parenteral inoculation, ingestion, and exposure to aerosols and infectious droplets have resulted in infection.

The agent may be present in lesion exudates, respiratory secretions, CSF, blood or lymph node aspirates from patients, tissues from infected animals, fluids from infected animals, and fluids from infected arthropods.
The agent can survive for months to years in carcasses, organs, and straw.
BSL-3 and ABSL-3 practices, containment equipment, and facilities are recommended.
Typhoid fever is a demonstrated hazard to laboratory personnel and students working with S. Typhi in teaching laboratories.
Ingestion and, less frequently, parenteral inoculation are the most significant modes of transmission in the laboratory.
Secondary transmission to other individuals outside of the laboratory is also a concern.

steps.text: Perform immediate and thorough cleansing following bites, scratches, splashes, or contact with potential fomites in high-risk areas.
Handle serum or blood products obtained through venipuncture carefully.

preconditions.text: If experiencing bites, scratches, splashes, or contact with potential fomites in high-risk areas

constraints.text: Proper use of engineering controls and personal protective equipment is essential to prevention.
Exposure via mucous membranes or skin breaks provides this agent access to a new host.
Contamination of needles via skin can occur.

steps.text: Receive another dose of IPV.
Destroy non-essential poliovirus materials.
Contain retained poliovirus material in certified poliovirus-essential facilities adhering to GAPIII measures.
Work with the U.S. National Authority for Containment (NAC) of Poliovirus at the CDC to achieve certification.

preconditions.text: Individual has had a primary series of OPV or IPV and is at an increased occupational risk.

constraints.text: Ingestion and parenteral inoculation are the primary routes of infection.
Infected non-human primates present a risk of infection.
Laboratory personnel and visitors with access to known poliovirus or infectious materials must have documented polio vaccination.
SE are highly toxic by intravenous and inhalation routes of exposure, with lethal doses causing death mainly due to shock and/or pulmonary edema.
Inhalation of less than 1 ng/kg can incapacitate more than 50% of exposed humans and the inhalation LD50 in humans may be as low as 20 ng/kg for SEB.

Exposure of mucous membranes to SEB in a laboratory setting or in clinical studies has been reported to cause conjunctivitis and localized cutaneous swelling, with some laboratory workers also experiencing incapacitating gastrointestinal symptoms.
Intradermal or dermal exposure to concentrated SE solutions or patch tests (≥ 1μg/cm2) has resulted in erythema, induration, or dermatitis.

steps.text: Obtain paired acute and convalescent sera from survivors for measurement of antibody response.
Inactivate solutions of ricin by treatment with sodium hypochlorite bleach.

constraints.text: Extend precautions to handling potentially contaminated clinical, diagnostic, and post-mortem samples because ricin may retain toxicity in lesion fluids, respiratory secretions, or unfixed tissues.
Handle purified preparations of natural ricin A-chain or B-chain and crude extracts from castor beans as if contaminated by ricin until proven otherwise by bioassay.
Use BSL-2 practices, containment equipment, and facilities, including laboratory coat, gloves, and eye protection, when handling ricin toxin or potentially contaminated materials.
Wear a full-face respirator if there is a potential for creating a toxin aerosol.
Use a BSC if there is any chance that ricin aerosols will be generated.

steps.text: Ensure that testing systems developed and used for each of the tests performed in the laboratory provide quality laboratory services for all aspects of test performance.

Ensure that the physical plant and environmental conditions of the laboratory are appropriate for the testing performed and provide a safe environment in which employees are protected from physical, chemical, and biological hazards.
Form a multidisciplinary team consisting of laboratory, infection prevention, and safety professionals to be responsible for the laboratory's risk assessments.
Document and routinely evaluate risk assessments.
Evaluate risk assessments when unanticipated or unusual events, near-misses, incidents, or accidents occur.

preconditions.text: New instruments, tests, staff, or processes have been added to the laboratory environment.
Unanticipated or unusual events, near-misses, incidents, or accidents occur.

constraints.text: Laboratory leadership should not conduct risk assessments alone.

steps.text: Conduct initial processing of clinical specimens for microbiology workup in a biosafety cabinet (BSC).

preconditions.text: Clinical laboratories handle blood and body fluids.

constraints.text: Follow OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens (BBP) Standard.
Maintain a written Exposure Control Plan (ECP) that addresses the identification, evaluation, and selection of effective engineering and work practice controls.
Implement Standard Precautions, treating all blood, body fluids, secretions, excretions (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes as containing transmissible infectious agents.
Conduct work at Biosafety Level 2 (BSL-2).
